claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 4 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 14 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 4 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 4 (under 16); Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 14 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start by setting up your assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! I'm ready to help you determine your Child Benefit eligibility. Let me start by asking you about your child.

**First, I need to know about your child's age:**

Child Benefit eligibility depends significantly on the child's age. Could you tell me:

1. **How old is your child?** (or if you have multiple children, let's start with one and their age)

This will help me determine which path to follow:
- Children **under 16** may automatically qualify
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Those **20 or over** generally don't qualify

What is your child's age?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I actually have two children I'd like to ask about - Alex who is 4 years old, and Blake who is 14.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can help you check eligibility for both Alex (4 years old) and Blake (14 years old).

Since both children are under 16, they fall into the category that may automatically qualify. However, I need to check a few more things to determine your eligibility for Child Benefit for each of them.

Let me continue with the assessment. For children under 16, the next question is important:

**Are either Alex or Blake working full-time (meaning 24 hours or more per week)?**

This is relevant because children under 16 who work full-time don't qualify for Child Benefit.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
To clarify: **Are both Alex (4) and Blake (14) working less than 24 hours per week, or not in paid work at all?**

Given their ages, I'd expect they're not working full-time, but I need to confirm this to proceed with the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them work at all. Alex is only 4, and Blake is still in school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that makes sense! Since neither Alex nor Blake are working, we can continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now the next question:

**Are either Alex or Blake receiving certain benefits in their own right?**

Specifically, I'm asking if either child is receiving:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA), or
- Universal Credit

in their own name (not as part of your household claim, but in their own right)?

Again, given their ages, I'd expect the answer is no, but I need to confirm this.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them receives any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let's continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now the next check:

**Are either Alex or Blake in an apprenticeship?**

This is relevant because certain types of apprenticeships can affect Child Benefit eligibility. The rules vary by region:
- In England: all apprenticeships disqualify
- In Scotland: Modern Apprenticeships disqualify
- In Wales: Foundation Apprenticeships are approved, but other apprenticeships disqualify

Given that Alex is 4 and Blake is 14 and still in school, I'm assuming neither is in an apprenticeship - is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct, neither of them is in an apprenticeship.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let's move forward.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now we need to check your residency status. This is an important requirement for Child Benefit.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You can qualify if ANY of the following apply to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status AND:
   - You're working and earning above the National Insurance threshold, OR
   - You're actively seeking work (for up to 91 days), OR
   - You have sufficient resources to support yourself and family, OR
   - You're studying with sufficient resources, OR
   - You're a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
4. You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

**Do any of these apply to you?** The most common situation is simply that you normally live in the UK - is that your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since you normally live in the UK, you meet the residency requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now I need to check if you're responsible for Alex and Blake.

**Are you responsible for both Alex and Blake?**

You're considered responsible if ANY of the following apply:

1. **You live with the children**
2. You contribute to their upkeep (paying at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards their care - food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents, etc.)
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

**Do you live with Alex and Blake?** That would be the most straightforward way to show responsibility.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I live with Blake, but Alex doesn't live with me. However, I do help out financially with Alex - I contribute towards their upkeep.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. That's helpful information.

So to confirm:
- **Blake (14)**: Lives with you ✓
- **Alex (4)**: Doesn't live with you, but you contribute financially to their upkeep

For Alex, since you contribute financially, I need to understand a bit more. You can be considered responsible if you pay at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount towards Alex's care. This can include things like:
- Food, clothes, pocket money
- Housing costs
- Presents
- Other contributions to their upkeep

These can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments.

**For Alex specifically: Do you contribute at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount (which is currently around £25 per week for the eldest child, or about £102-108 per month) towards their upkeep?**

And just to be clear - **where does Alex live?** Does Alex live in the UK with another parent or guardian?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I contribute about £50 a week towards Alex's upkeep - I help with clothes, food, and other things they need. Alex lives with my ex-partner here in the UK.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. So you do meet the responsibility requirement for Alex through your financial contributions (£50/week is well above the Child Benefit threshold).

Since you're responsible for both children, let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
This is an important question now:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake?**

This is particularly relevant for Alex, since they live with your ex-partner. 

- For **Alex**: Is your ex-partner currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?
- For **Blake**: Is anyone else claiming Child Benefit for Blake?

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child at a time. If someone else is already claiming, you would need to agree on who should be the claimant, or HMRC would need to make a decision if there's a dispute.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, my ex-partner isn't claiming Child Benefit for Alex. And nobody else is claiming for Blake either.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since no one else is claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake, we can continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check the living situation for both children.

**Are either Alex or Blake currently:**

1. **In hospital** (for an extended stay)?
2. **In local authority care** (looked after by the council)?
3. **In a foster care arrangement**?
4. Or are they **living normally** (either with family or in their usual home)?

From what you've told me:
- Blake lives with you
- Alex lives with your ex-partner

It sounds like both are living normally in family homes. Is that correct, or are there any special circumstances I should know about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's correct - both are living normally. Blake is at home with me, and Alex is living with my ex-partner. Neither of them is in hospital, care, or foster care.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let's continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Just one final check:

**Is the local council paying towards the accommodation or maintenance of either Alex or Blake?**

Generally, you cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child's upkeep. 

Given what you've told me - Blake lives with you and Alex lives with your ex-partner, and neither is in care - I'd expect the answer is no, but I need to confirm: **Is the council NOT paying towards either child's accommodation or maintenance?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything towards either of them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent news! Based on all the information you've provided, let me provide you with your eligibility assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Great News - You're ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit!

You meet all the eligibility requirements to claim Child Benefit for **both Alex and Blake**.

### Summary for Each Child:

**Alex (age 4):** ✅ **ELIGIBLE**
- Under 16 years old
- Not working
- You're responsible through your financial contributions (£50/week)
- Living with your ex-partner in the UK
- No one else is claiming for them
- Council not paying towards their maintenance

**Blake (age 14):** ✅ **ELIGIBLE**
- Under 16 years old
- Not working
- You're responsible as they live with you
- Living at home with you
- No one else is claiming for them
- Council not paying towards their maintenance

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and both children
3. **Provide birth certificates** or adoption certificates for Alex and Blake
4. **Payments:** Typically made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

### Important to Know:

⚠️ **High Income Child Benefit Charge:** Be aware that if you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay back some or all of the Child Benefit through the tax system. However, you can still claim it to protect your National Insurance credits.

You can apply here: **https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim**

Is there anything else you'd like to know about claiming Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex (age 4) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) Alex is under 16 years old, (2) Alex is not working, (3) Alex is not receiving benefits in their own right, (4) Alex is not in an apprenticeship, (5) The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), meeting residency requirements, (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex through financial contributions (£50/week towards upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit threshold), (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, (8) Alex is living normally with the claimant's ex-partner in the UK, and (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

Blake (age 14) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) Blake is under 16 years old, (2) Blake is not working, (3) Blake is not receiving benefits in their own right, (4) Blake is not in an apprenticeship, (5) The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), meeting residency requirements, (6) The claimant is responsible for Blake as they live together, (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake, (8) Blake is living normally at home with the claimant, and (9) The local council is not paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for both children, Alex (4) and Blake (14). Both children are under 16, not working, and living in the UK. The claimant meets UK residency requirements (living in Manchester) and has responsibility for both children - living with Blake and contributing financially to Alex's upkeep. No other person is claiming Child Benefit for either child, and the local council is not paying towards their maintenance. All eligibility criteria under the Child Benefit regulations are satisfied for both children.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (age 4) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) Alex is under 16 years old, (2) Alex is not working, (3) Alex is not receiving benefits in their own right, (4) Alex is not in an apprenticeship, (5) The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), meeting residency requirements, (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex through financial contributions (£50/week towards upkeep, which exceeds the Child Benefit threshold), (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, (8) Alex is living normally with the claimant's ex-partner in the UK, and (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

Blake (age 14) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) Blake is under 16 years old, (2) Blake is not working, (3) Blake is not receiving benefits in their own right, (4) Blake is not in an apprenticeship, (5) The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), meeting residency requirements, (6) The claimant is responsible for Blake as they live together, (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Blake, (8) Blake is living normally at home with the claimant, and (9) The local council is not paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for both children, Alex (4) and Blake (14). Both children are under 16, not working, and living in the UK. The claimant meets UK residency requirements (living in Manchester) and has responsibility for both children - living with Blake and contributing financially to Alex's upkeep. No other person is claiming Child Benefit for either child, and the local council is not paying towards their maintenance. All eligibility criteria under the Child Benefit regulations are satisfied for both children.
